llmWorkflowPrompt.purpose: Re-execute the repo's current workflow for producing the Silicon Data Remotion video whenever the source research documents change. Follow the existing architecture, keep the work deterministic, and regenerate the full Remotion artifact chain, including audio commentary, when the authoritative source documents are updated.
llmWorkflowPrompt.role: You are a coding assistant operating inside this repository. Your job is to read the current source documents, update the Silicon Data project implementation under projects/silicon-data/video/ plus the shared Remotion app under video/ when needed, update or regenerate the narration assets, run validation, and leave the repo in a reviewable state with a current rendered artifact.

file.role: selective source for closing asks, gating questions, and management-call framing

sourceUpdateTrigger.rule: Treat projects/silicon-data/sources/investment-committee-memo.md and projects/silicon-data/sources/management-diligence-request-list.md as the two root inputs that govern the entire derived artifact chain.
If either file changes materially, rerun the complete workflow end to end rather than only patching isolated files.

coreWorkflow.principle: Use projects/silicon-data/sources/investment-committee-memo.md and projects/silicon-data/sources/management-diligence-request-list.md exactly as they exist in the repo at the time of execution. Treat them as the authoritative inputs. Do not rewrite them or supplement them with outside research unless the user explicitly asks.
Do not build or maintain a generic Markdown-to-slides system for this repo. The workflow is manual editorial curation from prose into typed scene data.

Treat projects/silicon-data/video/data/scenes.ts as the primary implementation of the current narrative. Update that data first, then update composition and shared components only if the new content requires it.
Keep the video institutional, restrained, high-signal, and deterministic. This is an investor memo in motion, not a product promo.

Preserve the established visual direction: deep slate or warm off-black backgrounds, muted gold or copper accents, restrained typography, and low-amplitude motion. Prefer investor-research tone over startup-marketing tone.
Preserve the shared Remotion app under video/ and the Silicon Data project code under projects/silicon-data/video/. Update in place; do not rebuild the app from scratch.

This workflow includes audio commentary. Use Kokoro on CPU, keep narration scene-based, store narration inputs in projects/silicon-data/video/data/narration.json, generate one WAV per scene under projects/silicon-data/video/public/audio/, and use the mandated voice af_bella.

Use UV-managed Python tooling for narration generation. Keep the audio workflow rooted in pyproject.toml, uv.lock, and scripts/generate-audio.py. Use uv sync and the repo's pnpm project:audio -- --project silicon-data, pnpm project:dev -- --project silicon-data, pnpm project:render -- --project silicon-data, and pnpm project:smoke -- --project silicon-data commands as the standard Silicon Data execution path. Project render defaults and staged runtime asset kinds are defined in projects/silicon-data/video/project.config.json.

idempotenceRules.rule: Start by inspecting the current repo state, including uncommitted changes. Never revert unrelated user edits.
Compare the latest source documents against the current scene data and update only the scenes, citations, and layout details that are materially affected.

If the current implementation already reflects the source documents closely enough and the generated audio files plus final MP4 are current and present, make no source edits. Report a no-op result rather than churning formatting, copy, audio, or renders.
Keep stable file names, scene identifiers, component names, and composition IDs unless a change is genuinely required by the updated narrative.
Prefer editing existing files over creating new abstractions. Add new files or components only when the current structure cannot cleanly support the revised content.
Do not modify the source memo or diligence list unless the user explicitly asks you to rewrite those documents.
Do not change the repo workflow itself unless required. Update video/README.md or projects/silicon-data/v1-review-notes.md only if the workflow, scope choices, or review focus have actually changed.

If either primary source document changed materially, regenerate the entire artifact chain after updating code: narration data, scene WAV files, and the final rendered MP4. Do not limit the run to partial asset refreshes in that case.

For source-driven reruns, completion requires all derived artifacts to be brought back into sync with the updated sources. A source-driven run is not complete if it stops after code edits, narration edits, or audio generation without producing a fresh final render.

If the primary source documents did not change materially but the audio artifacts or rendered MP4 are missing, stale, or inconsistent with the current implementation, regenerate the missing or stale artifacts without unnecessary source edits.

scene.purpose: Establish company, date, recommendation, and the gating issues immediately.
Frame the benchmark-plus-marketplace thesis and why institutional distribution matters.
scene.delivery: Text-led thesis framing with concise supporting points.

scene.purpose: Show visible product, distribution, and press signal without overstating validation.
scene.delivery: Timeline or milestone layout that emphasizes dated proof points.

scene.purpose: Explain the strategic logic, competitive field, and operating thinness.
scene.delivery: Structured context view with short explanatory captions.

source.section: Core risk #1: entity structure, regulatory ambiguity, and forward-contract/derivatives positioning

scene.purpose: Show the largest gating issue around entity structure, exemptions, and the path to regulated products.
scene.delivery: Risk-panel treatment with highlighted contradictions, legal-map gaps, and roadmap questions.

source.section: Core risk #3: conflicts from proprietary-trading-firm parentage

scene.purpose: Frame the DRW and Jump conflict stack as a governance and counterparty issue, not just a branding detail.
scene.delivery: Risk-panel treatment with information-barrier framing, control questions, and controlled animation.

source.section: Core risk #2: index methodology governance, IOSCO readiness, and benchmark independence
Core risk #4: market formation, competitive durability, and runway

scene.purpose: Show benchmark-governance proof gaps, replication needs, and the market-formation burden.
scene.delivery: Risk-panel treatment emphasizing missing proof, validator gaps, and durability evidence.

scene.purpose: Close on the recommendation and present the hardest next-step asks for management.
scene.delivery: Closing recommendation plus selective diligence asks centered on legal mapping, replication, conflict governance, traction, and runway.

executionPlan.step: Read the two source documents, the build plan, the review notes, and the existing files under projects/silicon-data/video/ plus shared files under video/src/. Also inspect the audio plan, canonical audio artifacts under projects/silicon-data/video/public/audio/, staged audio under video/public/audio/silicon-data/, and the current rendered output. Inspect git status before editing so you do not overwrite unrelated work.

Compare the latest memo and diligence list against the current implementation. Identify which scene claims, dates, figures, questions, citations, narration lines, and output artifacts changed. Do not assume every scene needs rewriting, but do determine whether a full artifact regeneration is required. If either root source changed materially, mark the run as a full workflow rerun.
Edit projects/silicon-data/video/data/scenes.ts first. Keep the narrative concise and investor-facing. Preserve existing scene IDs where possible so downstream code stays stable.
Edit projects/silicon-data/video/data/citations.ts to keep each scene's citation footer aligned with the revised claims and sources.
Update the narration source and any typed narration wrapper so the audio commentary matches the latest source documents, scene boundaries, and the mandated af_bella voice policy.

step.name: Adjust rendering and audio wiring only if needed

executionPlan.step: Update projects/silicon-data/video/compositions/SiliconDataIcV1.tsx, video/src/components/, video/src/theme.ts, video/src/utils/, and the audio wiring only when the revised copy no longer fits, the visual hierarchy needs to change, or the scene structure genuinely changed.

Keep the video text-led and diagram-led. Do not introduce live data visualization, generic slide generation, or unrelated product features. Continue using citations, restrained motion, and one narration file per scene.

Target roughly 105 to 120 seconds at 30 fps. Prefer tightening copy before changing scene durations. Change durations only when the updated narrative or narration cannot fit cleanly, and explain any timing changes.

Use the repo's UV-managed Python workflow to sync dependencies and regenerate the scene narration WAV files under projects/silicon-data/video/public/audio/. The shared wrapper should then restage those assets into video/public/audio/silicon-data/. When the primary source documents changed materially, regenerate all scene audio files, not just a subset.
Run the project checks and render the main composition after edits and audio regeneration. If a check fails, fix the issue or report the blocker clearly.

For source-driven reruns, confirm that the updated source documents, typed scene data, narration data, generated scene audio files, and final MP4 all correspond to the same current repo state before concluding the task.

file.expectation: Primary typed scene content. Update first.
Scene-level citation labels. Keep aligned with source claims.
Primary narration source. Keep aligned with current scenes and af_bella voice policy.
Typed narration wrapper for Remotion. Keep aligned with current scene ids.
Main composition sequencing and scene selection.
Reusable scene building blocks. Touch only if content or layout requires it.
Visual system. Keep institutional tone and low churn.
Composition registration. Keep stable unless composition contract changes.
CPU Kokoro narration generator. Keep runnable through UV and default to af_bella.
Repo-root Python dependency definition for the audio tooling.
Committed Python lockfile for reproducible audio generation.
Canonical generated scene WAV artifacts. Regenerate when sources change materially.
Derived staged audio for Remotion runtime. Rebuild through the shared wrapper.
Commands and workflow notes. Update only if workflow changes.
Scope and review focus. Update only if those choices change.

qualityBar.item: Recommendation framing must remain clear in the opening and closing.
Claims, dates, figures, and partner references must match the latest source documents.
The legal-map, governance, and conflict questions must remain legible and ordered by investment importance.
The closing scene must reflect the most relevant diligence asks for the next management call.
The visual result must feel institutional and reviewable, not promotional.
The narration must track the current source documents, fit scene timing, and use af_bella consistently.
The generated scene WAV files and the final MP4 must be refreshed when the primary sources change materially.
A material change to either root source file must trigger a complete workflow rerun from editorial data through final render.
The final code should stay simple, typed, and easy to revise on the next iteration.

finalResponseContract.item: Summarize the source changes you detected.
State explicitly whether either root source file changed materially and whether that triggered a full workflow rerun.
List the files you changed and why.
Report whether the run was a true update or an intentional no-op.
Report whether you regenerated the full artifact chain or only refreshed missing or stale artifacts, and why.
Report the narration and audio outputs you generated.
Report the validation commands you ran and their outcomes.
Call out any remaining review risks, especially around editorial judgment or timing.
